turns.content: I need to vent about something cos if I dont I might actually scream
Go for it. I'm all ears. What happened?
boiler install today. was supposed to be a straightforward swap. old combi out, new combi in, same location same pipework. quoted the customer 6 hours. just got home. twelve hours mate. TWELVE
Twelve hours on what was supposed to be a straight swap? That's brutal. What went wrong -- was it the pipework, the flue, or something nobody could have predicted?

everything. literally everything. turned up at 8am, got the old boiler off the wall by 9. all good so far. then I look at the pipework behind it and its copper from about 1985, half of its green, theres a joint thats been bodged with plumbers mate instead of being soldered properly. whoever did the last install should be banned from touching pipes

Oh no. The classic "what's behind the boiler" surprise. Plumber's mate on a joint instead of a proper solder -- that's someone who either didn't know what they were doing or didn't care. Either way, you're the one stuck dealing with it 40 years later.

So you had to redo all the pipework behind it?

had to strip out about 2 metres of copper and redo it all. new joints, new fittings, the lot. that alone took me 3 hours cos the runs were tight behind the kitchen units. then I get the new boiler up on the wall and the bracket positions dont line up with the old fixing holes so I'm drilling fresh holes into the brickwork

Of course they don't line up. Different manufacturer, different bracket spacing -- but you never know until you're holding it up against the wall. The tight runs behind kitchen units are the worst too -- you're working with your arms above your head in a space designed for mice, not people.

Please tell me the flue was straightforward at least.

HAHA no. the old flue hole was too low for the new boiler position so I had to core drill a new one through the outside wall. except the wall turned out to be double brick with a cavity full of rubble. took me 45 minutes just to get through it. dust everywhere, customer is hovering behind me asking if everythings ok every ten minutes

The hovering customer while you're covered in brick dust and things are going sideways -- that's the worst combination. You're trying to concentrate on not destroying their wall and they're there going "so is it nearly done?"

Double brick with rubble-filled cavity sounds like the house was built in an era when building standards were more of a suggestion than a rule. How'd the flue seal go once you finally got through?

flue went in fine once the hole was right. sealed it up proper, fire cement and silicone, no draughts. that part was actually satisfying. but then I'm connecting the gas and I realise the isolation valve underneath is seized solid. wont budge. so thats another 40 minutes swapping that out

A seized isolation valve is just the cherry on top at that point. At least it's a fixable problem, not a "call the gas board" problem. Sounds like every single component in that system was conspiring against you.

Once the gas was connected and the new valve was in, did the commissioning go smoothly or did the boiler have its own ideas?

commissioning actually went alright thankfully. fired up first time, pressure held, no leaks, hot water and heating both working. I stood there watching the pressure gauge for about 10 minutes just making sure cos at that point I didnt trust anything in that house. did all the paperwork, benchmark certificate, gas safety record, the lot

That moment when the boiler fires up after a day like that and the pressure holds steady -- there's nothing quite like it. Pure relief. And fair play for standing there watching the gauge; after everything that went wrong, a healthy dose of paranoia is completely justified.

Good that you did all the paperwork properly too. Some engineers would rush that part after a 12-hour day but that's the bit that protects you and the customer. Did you have to adjust the quote, or are you eating the extra hours?

I charged extra for the pipework cos thats materials and labour I couldnt have predicted. but the rest of it I just ate cos its not the customers fault their walls are rubbish. worked out I probably made about 8 quid an hour today haha

Eight quid an hour -- you'd earn more stacking shelves! But that's the reality of fixed-price quoting on older properties. You did the right thing only charging for the genuinely unforeseeable stuff. The customer will respect that and probably recommend you to everyone they know, which is worth more in the long run.

Are you going to adjust how you quote for older properties going forward? Maybe a clause for hidden pipework or unexpected structural issues?

yeah I think I need to start adding a line on my quotes that says additional work may be required if existing pipework or fittings are not up to standard. or just charge more upfront for houses built before 1990. either way todays one of those days where you question your career choices haha. anyway I'm done. having a cup of tea and not thinking about pipes until tomorrow

You've earned that cup of tea and then some. A disclaimer line on your quotes is a smart move -- something like "Quote based on standard installation. Additional charges may apply if existing pipework, fittings, or structural issues require remedial work." Protects you without scaring the customer.

Enjoy the tea, put your feet up, and try to appreciate that despite everything, the boiler fired up first time and the customer has heating tonight. That's a win even if it doesn't feel like one right now.
